idioms.definition: To pay too much attention to small, unimportant details; to argue about trivial differences that make no practical difference
examples: You're splitting hairs – both versions say essentially the same thing.
Instead of splitting hairs over minor wording, let's focus on the bigger issues.
They spent an hour splitting hairs about whether to call it a 'policy' or a 'guideline'.
idioms.usageTip: Often used critically, suggesting someone is being overly pedantic. Can also be used as a noun: 'hair-splitting'. Common in debates, writing, and academic discussions.

idioms.definition: Quick or slow to understand or grasp something
examples: She's very quick on the uptake – explain it once and she understands immediately.
He's a bit slow on the uptake sometimes, but once he gets it, he never forgets.
Don't worry if you're slow on the uptake at first; this is genuinely complex material.
idioms.usageTip: Can be used positively ('quick on the uptake') or as mild criticism ('slow on the uptake'). 'Uptake' here means the ability to understand or learn something quickly.

idioms.definition: To waste time and effort creating something that already exists; to do unnecessary work by starting from scratch
examples: Why reinvent the wheel? Use the template the team developed last year.
The new manager tried to reinvent the wheel instead of building on what had already worked.
There's no point reinventing the wheel when there's already an established, proven solution.
idioms.usageTip: Usually used negatively, suggesting inefficiency or poor use of resources. Very common in business, project management, and technology contexts.

idioms.idiom: it's as broad as it's long
idioms.definition: Used to say that two choices or options are equally good, bad, or difficult; it makes no practical difference which you choose
examples: Should we go by train or bus? It's as broad as it's long – they take exactly the same time.
Paying monthly or annually? It's as broad as it's long if the total cost works out the same.
Whether we start Monday or Tuesday, it's as broad as it's long – we'll finish at the same point.
idioms.usageTip: Mainly British English. Equivalent to 'it's six of one and half a dozen of the other'. Used when two options are truly equivalent in outcome.

idioms.definition: To try to have the same possessions, lifestyle, or social status as your neighbours or peers, often at personal cost
examples: They bought a bigger car just to keep up with the Joneses next door.
Trying to keep up with the Joneses led them into serious financial debt.
She refused to keep up with the Joneses and chose to live simply and within her means.
idioms.usageTip: Often used critically. Suggests materialism or unhealthy social pressure. The 'Joneses' is a generic surname representing wealthy or successful neighbours or peers.

idioms.definition: To accept other people's ways of living, beliefs, or choices even if they are different from your own
examples: I don't agree with their lifestyle, but I believe in live and let live.
A live-and-let-live attitude is essential in any diverse and tolerant society.
My grandmother always said: live and let live – it's not your place to judge.
idioms.usageTip: Used to express a tolerant attitude towards others. Can also be used as a compound adjective: 'a live-and-let-live philosophy'. Common in discussions of diversity, culture, and personal freedom.

fillInExercises.sentence: You're ___ – whether we call it a 'delay' or a 'postponement', the meeting isn't happening tomorrow.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Split hairs' means to argue about trivial or unimportant differences that have no practical effect.

fillInExercises.sentence: She was ___ and understood the new system after just one brief demonstration.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Quick on the uptake' means fast to understand or grasp something.

fillInExercises.sentence: There's no need to ___; the previous team already developed a perfectly good solution to this problem.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Reinvent the wheel' means to waste effort creating something that already exists and works.

fillInExercises.sentence: We could hire a freelancer or use the in-house team – honestly, ___; the cost works out the same either way.
options: it's as broad as it's long
fillInExercises.correct: it's as broad as it's long
fillInExercises.explanation: 'It's as broad as it's long' means two options are equivalent – neither is better or worse than the other.

fillInExercises.sentence: They spent far beyond their means trying to ___ in their wealthy neighbourhood.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Keep up with the Joneses' means to try to match the lifestyle or possessions of your peers, often recklessly.

fillInExercises.sentence: She might do things very differently from me, but my philosophy has always been to ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Live and let live' means to accept other people's choices and ways of living, even when you personally disagree.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Look, I know we have different opinions on this, but I try to [live and let live|live and let live] when it comes to working styles.

lines.speaker: Lisa
lines.text: I appreciate that. But I do think the team is [splitting hairs|split hairs] over which project management tool to use – they both do the same job.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Exactly. Whether we use one tool or the other, [it's as broad as it's long|it's as broad as it's long].

lines.speaker: Lisa
lines.text: I suggested we use the framework from last year, but Marcus wants to [reinvent the wheel|reinvent the wheel] entirely.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: He's not always [quick on the uptake|quick/slow on the uptake] when it comes to efficiency. He prefers doing things his own way.

lines.speaker: Lisa
lines.text: Part of it is status, I think. He wants to [keep up with the Joneses|keep up with the Joneses] at the leadership level.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Well, we can't afford to waste time. I'll have a word with him.

lines.speaker: Lisa
lines.text: Thanks. We really need to move forward, not debate endlessly.

lines.speaker: Dave
lines.text: My new neighbours renovated their entire garden last month. Now I feel the pressure to do something – though I really don't want to be [keeping up with the Joneses|keep up with the Joneses].

lines.speaker: Nora
lines.text: Why worry about it? [Live and let live|live and let live] – your garden suits your lifestyle perfectly.

lines.speaker: Dave
lines.text: The thing is, my partner and I keep arguing about the design. She wants a completely new layout; I think a few plants will do the job.

lines.speaker: Nora
lines.text: Sounds like you're both [splitting hairs|split hairs]. Either option will improve the space.

lines.speaker: Dave
lines.text: You're right. Whether we replant the borders or add a patio, [it's as broad as it's long|it's as broad as it's long].

lines.speaker: Nora
lines.text: Just don't [reinvent the wheel|reinvent the wheel] – there are thousands of good garden designs online already.

lines.speaker: Dave
lines.text: Ha! Very true. My partner is quite [quick on the uptake|quick/slow on the uptake] with interior design – she'll find something brilliant in no time.

lines.speaker: Nora
lines.text: Perfect. Sounds like the problem's already solved then.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: argue about trivial or unimportant differences
fast or slow to understand something
waste time creating something that already exists

pairs.idiom: it's as broad as it's long
pairs.meaning: two options are equally good or bad
try to match the lifestyle or possessions of your peers
accept other people's ways of living even if different from your own

quizExercises.sentence: The team spent an hour debating whether to call it a 'guideline' or a 'rule' – they were just ___.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Split hairs' means to argue about trivial differences that have no meaningful impact on the outcome.

quizExercises.sentence: There's a perfectly good system already in place – there's absolutely no reason to ___.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Reinvent the wheel' means to waste effort and time developing something that already exists and functions well.

quizExercises.idiom: it's as broad as it's long
quizExercises.question: A colleague cannot decide whether to take the morning train or the afternoon train to a conference – both arrive with plenty of time to spare. What would you say?
options: It's as broad as it's long – either train works perfectly fine
You're reinventing the wheel by taking the train
You need to live and let live about this decision
quizExercises.explanation: 'It's as broad as it's long' means two choices are equivalent – neither option is better than the other.

quizExercises.question: A family takes out a large loan to buy a luxury car they cannot afford, mainly because their neighbours just bought a similar one. Which idiom best describes their behaviour?
quizExercises.explanation: 'Keep up with the Joneses' means trying to match the lifestyle or status symbols of your peers, often at significant personal cost.
